title: Schema Migration Release Coordination
description: A release-and-rollout control workflow that manages promotion waves, rollback readiness, and deployment dependencies across environments. Focus case: schema migration release coordination.
assumptions: Promotion channels model coordination domains (not individual deployments); per-service relations remain per-service to avoid arity mismatches.
Unary availability predicates (tools, backups, configs) indicate global availability of that artifact; binary relations indicate assignment to a channel.
Operator credentials and backup artifacts are consumed or toggled by actions to reflect temporary claims and releases.
Initial connectivity (which services/environments a channel can target) is modeled in the init relation maps and remains static for the scenario.
No action names use the lexical root 'release' to prevent confusion with promotion_channel semantics; terminology uses 'promotion', 'channel', 'stage', and 'promote'.
risks: Misinterpreting unary predicates as per-service could produce incorrect automation; maintain arity discipline between availability flags and per-service bindings.
Collapsing distinct abstract symbols into the same real-world concept would break injectivity — keep channel, channel endpoint variants, and campaign-group types distinct.
Insufficiently explicit rollback gating (e.g., missing backup snapshots or operator approvals) can allow finalization actions to proceed prematurely; ensure safety flags are enforced by tooling.
Naming inconsistency between channel capabilities (e.g., supports_direct_finalize vs supports_versioned_finalize) may confuse operators—document capability semantics clearly.
Complex interdependencies (prechecks, backups, operator creds, environment readiness) increase plan fragility; plan runtime checks and observable state transitions for automation.
